title: Security force official in blue uniform shot at my face: Protestor with pellet wounds
author: Shahid Tantray
content_html:
ON 20 JULY 2026, Shaikh Irshad Mansoori was with a group of protesters at Delhi’s Connaught Place. In front of a giant charkha, the group was raising slogans against the government. Mansoori told me that he was part of the protest called by the Cockroach Janata Party, a collective that had initiated a march to parliament demanding, among other things, the resignation of the union education minister, Dharmendra Pradhan. The massive crowd was conducting its affairs peacefully and in pursuit of its primary objective—demanding Pradhan’s resignation.

At around 5 pm, Mansoori said that an officer in a blue uniform, standing around fifty meters away, aimed at his face with a gun and shot him. “Its particles, its pellets, its pieces penetrated all over my face,” he said, adding that it also struck him in his neck and shoulders. “And suddenly there I was bleeding a lot from my face, my shoulders and my neck,” he said. The Rapid Action Force, which comes under the Central Reserve Police Force, and which was deployed that day, dons a blue uniform.

The 25-year-old from Madhya Pradesh stood there in a state of shock. People nearby helped him. “They placed some cloth on my face, and they suddenly stopped a vehicle, like a bike, so that they could take me to the emergency ward,” Mansoori said. They took him to Lady Hardinge Medical College, where he is currently being treated for his injuries.

Hundreds of security personnel, including the Delhi Police, as well as the Rapid Action Force, which comes under the Central Reserve Police Force, were deployed in the area that day. The RAF dons a blue uniform. Through that day, the Delhi Police had brutally cracked down on the crowds of thousands who had gathered to march to the parliament. Dozens of protestors told The Caravan that the police lathi-charged students and lobbed tear gas shells at them, even though the march was peaceful. The government had shut down internet services in the area, leaving the protestors unable to communicate or reach out for help. But videos of police brutality soon began circulating widely, with many protesters filming as the police attacked or beat them. According to media reports, around eighty protesters were injured and had to be admitted for treatment in hospitals across the city.
content_text:
ON 20 JULY 2026
, Shaikh Irshad Mansoori was with a group of protesters at Delhi’s Connaught Place. In front of a giant charkha, the group was raising slogans against the government. Mansoori told me that he was part of the protest called by the Cockroach Janata Party, a collective that had initiated a march to parliament demanding, among other things, the resignation of the union education minister, Dharmendra Pradhan. The massive crowd was conducting its affairs peacefully and in pursuit of its primary objective—demanding Pradhan’s resignation.
At around 5 pm, Mansoori said that an officer in a blue uniform, standing around fifty meters away, aimed at his face with a gun and shot him. “Its particles, its pellets, its pieces penetrated all over my face,” he said, adding that it also struck him in his neck and shoulders. “And suddenly there I was bleeding a lot from my face, my shoulders and my neck,” he said. The Rapid Action Force, which comes under the Central Reserve Police Force, and which was deployed that day, dons a blue uniform.
The 25-year-old from Madhya Pradesh stood there in a state of shock. People nearby helped him. “They placed some cloth on my face, and they suddenly stopped a vehicle, like a bike, so that they could take me to the emergency ward,” Mansoori said. They took him to Lady Hardinge Medical College, where he is currently being treated for his injuries.
Hundreds of security personnel, including the Delhi Police, as well as the Rapid Action Force, which comes under the Central Reserve Police Force, were deployed in the area that day. The RAF dons a blue uniform. Through that day, the Delhi Police had brutally cracked down on the crowds of thousands who had gathered to march to the parliament. Dozens of protestors
told
The Caravan
that the police lathi-charged students and lobbed tear gas shells at them, even though the march was peaceful. The government had shut down internet services in the area, leaving the protestors unable to communicate or reach out for help. But videos of police brutality soon began circulating widely, with many protesters filming as the police attacked or beat them. According to media reports, around eighty protesters were injured and had to be admitted for treatment in hospitals across the city.

title: The rise of Narendra Modi
author: Vinod K Jose
content_html:
ON THE AFTERNOON OF 22 APRIL 1498, a few kilometres off the shore of the East African port of Malindi, Captain-Major Vasco da Gama was a happy man. After drifting for four frustrating months up the continent’s southeastern coast, from Mozambique to Mombasa, facing the hostility of local rulers and Arab and African merchants, the Portuguese captain had finally found a navigator who could take him to India.

The man who steered da Gama across the Indian Ocean to Calicut—and into the history books as the “discoverer” of the ocean route to Asia—was a Gujarati named Kanji Malam. A trader of cotton and indigo from Kutch, Malam made regular voyages to the African coast to barter his goods for gold and ivory.

That it was a Gujarati who led da Gama to India should come as no surprise. The Gujarati aptitude for navigation, seafaring and commerce was already legendary, and Gujarati merchants had established trading routes stretching from the Persian Gulf to present-day Malaysia and Indonesia.

For two millennia before the Portuguese arrived, Gujarat had been at the junction of the world’s two main trading axes: the Silk Road and the Spice Route, and it was a principal distribution hub for goods from African, Arab and Asian ports entering the subcontinent. From the coast, one inland trade route went east to Bihar; another north to Mathura; and a third south to Marathwada. Merchants from Greece, Arabia, Persia, Africa and China came to do business in Gujarat centuries before European explorers set foot in India.
content_text:
ON THE AFTERNOON OF 22 APRIL 1498
, a few kilometres off the shore of the East African port of Malindi, Captain-Major Vasco da Gama was a happy man. After drifting for four frustrating months up the continent’s southeastern coast, from Mozambique to Mombasa, facing the hostility of local rulers and Arab and African merchants, the Portuguese captain had finally found a navigator who could take him to India.
The man who steered da Gama across the Indian Ocean to Calicut—and into the history books as the “discoverer” of the ocean route to Asia—was a Gujarati named Kanji Malam. A trader of cotton and indigo from Kutch, Malam made regular voyages to the African coast to barter his goods for gold and ivory.
That it was a Gujarati who led da Gama to India should come as no surprise. The Gujarati aptitude for navigation, seafaring and commerce was already legendary, and Gujarati merchants had established trading routes stretching from the Persian Gulf to present-day Malaysia and Indonesia.
For two millennia before the Portuguese arrived, Gujarat had been at the junction of the world’s two main trading axes: the Silk Road and the Spice Route, and it was a principal distribution hub for goods from African, Arab and Asian ports entering the subcontinent. From the coast, one inland trade route went east to Bihar; another north to Mathura; and a third south to Marathwada. Merchants from Greece, Arabia, Persia, Africa and China came to do business in Gujarat centuries before European explorers set foot in India.

title: Selections from an anthology of writing on and by Umar Khalid “Partly, this book also emerges from our habit of gathering books for Umar to read while he is ‘inside’ (because the one thing he has more than us is time),” the editors note in their introduction. “Partly, it is also to see his incarceration as a lens through which we attempt to understand the time that we are all living through.”
author: Anirban Bhattacharya,Banojyotsna Lahiri,Shuddhabrata Sengupta
content_html:
Since 13 September 2020, the activist, historian and former student leader Umar Khalid has been jailed, charged under several sections of the Unlawful Activities (Prevention) Act. This excerpt includes selections from the introductory chapter of Umar Khalid and His World: An Anthology, a volume edited by Anirban Bhattacharya, Banojyotsna Lahiri and Shuddhabrata Sengupta, and published by Three Essays Collective. The collection highlights questions and ideas that animated Umar and his work, before and after his arrest. It also compiles many of his articles, letters and “stray thoughts, on the passing of time, and on the difficulty of the passing of time while incarcerated.”

“Partly, this book also emerges from our habit of gathering books for Umar to read while he is ‘inside’ (because the one thing he has more than us is time),” the editors note in their introduction to the book. “Partly, it is also to see his incarceration as a lens through which we attempt to understand the time that we are all living through.”

FROM A HANDWRITTEN LIST of books that we have managed to collect for him over these years, which Umar has marked as “read,” we know that his reading habit has grown more voracious and eclectic. That he reads whatever he can lay his hands on. Some of these books also make their way back from prison, when Umar’s small cell (eight feet in width and 24 feet in length) becomes overfull with books and printed material. The cell was originally meant for three inmates, but he occupies it alone owing to security concerns. Then, the books make their way back to Banojyotsna’s apartment. And wait (once read, yet desirous of being read again) to be reunited with their primary reader, when he finally walks out of confinement.
content_text:
Since 13 September 2020, the activist, historian and former student leader Umar Khalid has been jailed, charged under several sections of the Unlawful Activities (Prevention) Act. This excerpt includes selections from the introductory chapter of
Umar Khalid and His World: An Anthology
, a volume edited by Anirban Bhattacharya, Banojyotsna Lahiri and Shuddhabrata Sengupta, and published by Three Essays Collective. The collection highlights questions and ideas that animated Umar and his work, before and after his arrest. It also compiles many of his articles, letters and “stray thoughts, on the passing of time, and on the difficulty of the passing of time while incarcerated.”
“Partly, this book also emerges from our habit of gathering books for Umar to read while he is ‘inside’ (because the one thing he has more than us is time),” the editors note in their introduction to the book. “Partly, it is also to see his incarceration as a lens through which we attempt to understand the time that we are all living through.”
FROM A HANDWRITTEN
LIST
of books that we have managed to collect for him over these years, which Umar has marked as “read,” we know that his reading habit has grown more voracious and eclectic. That he reads whatever he can lay his hands on. Some of these books also make their way back from prison, when Umar’s small cell (eight feet in width and 24 feet in length) becomes overfull with books and printed material. The cell was originally meant for three inmates, but he occupies it alone owing to security concerns. Then, the books make their way back to Banojyotsna’s apartment. And wait (once read, yet desirous of being read again) to be reunited with their primary reader, when he finally walks out of confinement.
